story:
My name is Ibrahim Mwangi and I coordinate orphan care programs in Kibera, Nairobi, Kenya, through the Amani Children's Foundation. Since January 2023, we have been providing shelter, daily meals, school fees, and emotional support to 47 orphaned children aged 4 to 16 whose parents died from tuberculosis, HIV/AIDS-related complications, and road accidents along the Mombasa Highway.

Kibera is one of sub-Saharan Africa's largest informal settlements, with an estimated population of over 250,000 people. According to UNICEF Kenya's 2022 household survey, the ward has an orphan-to-caregiver ratio of 1:9 — among the highest in Nairobi County. Our children attend Makina Primary School and St. Joseph's Secondary School; we pay KES 12,000 per child per year for public school levies, uniforms, and textbooks.

In March 2024, three of our residential staff left due to funding shortfalls, reducing our supervised capacity from 47 to 30 children. The remaining 17 children — currently with temporary host families in Langa'ta — need to return to the foundation by August 2024 before the school year resumes. We require KES 1,850,000 (approximately USD 14,300) to cover: rehabilitation of the eastern dormitory roof damaged by the April rains (KES 480,000), six months of nutritional support at KES 8,500 per child per month for 47 children (KES 2,397,000 — partially covered by USAID Food for Peace sub-grant), staff salaries for four childcare workers (KES 320,000 for six months), and educational supplies for the September 2024 intake (KES 95,000).

Amani Children's Foundation is registered with Kenya's NGO Coordination Board under certificate NGO/R/3/14-052 issued 11 February 2016. Our 2023 annual report, audited by Mbugua & Associates CPAs, Nairobi, is available on request. Every shilling raised through this campaign will be disbursed under the oversight of our board chair, Dr. Fatuma Odhiambo (pediatrician, Kenyatta National Hospital), and verified by our external auditor.
vouching_narrative:
Amani Children's Foundation has been a partner of the Kibera Muslim Community Network (KMCN) since 2019. Sheikh Yusuf Abdalla Hassan, resident imam of Masjid Al-Noor, Makina Ward, Kibera (tel: +254 722 ███ ███, masjidalnoor.kibera@gmail.com), has personally visited the foundation's premises on four occasions and can confirm the presence and living conditions of the children. Sheikh Yusuf delivered Friday khutbahs in support of the 2022 and 2023 fundraising drives and collected sadaqah on the foundation's behalf, disbursing KES 340,000 in 2023. His written endorsement letter dated 14 April 2024, stamped with the mosque's official seal, is included in our documentation package.

In addition, Catholic Relief Services Kenya (CRS-Kenya), Nairobi field office, Hurlingham, has maintained a Memorandum of Understanding with Amani since July 2021 (MoU reference CRS-KE-2021-0047). CRS-Kenya Program Officer Ms. Beatrice Anyango (banyango@crs.org) conducted a site verification visit on 7 February 2024 and can confirm enrollment figures, staffing levels, and facility conditions. CRS-Kenya co-funded the 2023 dormitory fire-safety upgrades under their Regional Child Protection Initiative.

Both Sheikh Yusuf and Ms. Anyango are willing to be contacted directly by LaunchGood's Trust & Safety team for verification.
